title: pondeR • Statistical Analysis Made Easier
text: pondeR R package providing curated collection functions generating publication-ready descriptive inferential statistics tables figures — minimal coding overhead. built upon robust gtsummary framework leverages power tidyverse. package aims make statistical analysis accessible everyone, ensuring transparency analytical methods providing consistent template results.

title: pondeR • Statistical Analysis Made Easier
text: pondeR offers comprehensive suite tools various stages data analysis general statistical reporting: Summarizing Data & Checking Distributions: Generate publication-ready summary tables, calculate anthropometric indices according standards, test normality skewness. Comparing Groups & Identifying Associations: Provides methods detecting differences cohorts, calculating fold changes, testing statistical associations. Includes plotting functions like volcano plots heatmaps. Predictive Modeling & Classification: Offers tools regularized regression (Ridge, LASSO, Elastic Net), Linear Mixed-Effect Models, Logistic regression analysis performance evaluation (AUC/AUROC) automated bias correction options. Multivariate Exploration Discrimination: Features dimensionality reduction visualization tools (PCA, PLS, PCoA) simplified exploration complex datasets, including scree score plots. Plotting Functions: variety plotting utilities, including distribution plots, mean maps, multi-Y plots. Export Functions: Easily export data frames Excel/CSV files figures PNGs. Utilities & Helpers: collection utility functions data management, relabelling, randomization.

title: pondeR • Statistical Analysis Made Easier

code: # Install pak if you haven't already install.packages("pak")  # Install the GitHub package pak::pak("jllcalorio/pondeR")

title: pondeR • Statistical Analysis Made Easier
text: ’s quick example generate descriptive summary table using pondeR (assuming data frame named my_data): (Note: exact arguments run_summarytable may vary. Please refer package documentation detailed usage.)
code: library(pondeR) library(dplyr) # Often useful with gtsummary-based packages  # Example data (replace with your actual data) my_data <- tibble::tibble(   age = rnorm(100, 30, 5),   gender = sample(c("Male", "Female"), 100, replace = TRUE),   treatment = sample(c("A", "B", "C"), 100, replace = TRUE),   score = runif(100, 0, 100) )  # Generate a summary table my_data %>%   run_summarytable(     # Specify variables and grouping if needed     include = c(age, gender, score),     by = treatment   )

title: Get Features with Non-Zero Coefficients from a Regularized Regression Result — get_sigfeatures
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ ## ---- Setup --------------------------------------------------------------- set.seed(42) n  <- 80 p  <- 30  feat <- as.data.frame(   matrix(rnorm(n * p), nrow = n,          dimnames = list(NULL, paste0("feat_", seq_len(p)))) ) meta <- data.frame(   Group = sample(c("Control", "Case"), n, replace = TRUE) )  ## ---- Binary classification ----------------------------------------------- res_bin <- run_regreg(   x    = feat, metadata = meta,   pred = "Group", ref = "Control",   alpha = c(0, 0.5, 1), seed = 123 )  # Best model features (default) get_sigfeatures(res_bin)  # Features from LASSO specifically get_sigfeatures(res_bin, alpha = 1)  # Sorted by absolute coefficient magnitude get_sigfeatures(res_bin, sort_by = "coef")  # Include the intercept term get_sigfeatures(res_bin, include_intercept = TRUE)  ## ---- Multinomial classification ------------------------------------------ meta3 <- data.frame(   Group = sample(c("A", "B", "C"), n, replace = TRUE) ) res_multi <- run_regreg(   x    = feat, metadata = meta3,   pred = "Group", ref = "A",   alpha = 0.5, seed = 1 )  # Union of non-zero features across all comparisons get_sigfeatures(res_multi)  # Per-comparison breakdown get_sigfeatures(res_multi, by_comparison = TRUE)  ## ---- Gaussian regression ------------------------------------------------- meta_num <- data.frame(Score = rnorm(n)) res_num  <- run_regreg(   x    = feat, metadata = meta_num,   pred = "Score", alpha = 0.5, seed = 7 ) get_sigfeatures(res_num) } # }

what: Check if an Object is a Tabular Data Structure — is_tabular
title: Check if an Object is a Tabular Data Structure — is_tabular
text: Tests whether object belongs recognized tabular data class - data.frame, tbl_df (tibble), data.table, matrix - confirms exactly two dimensions. Returns single logical value (TRUE FALSE).

title: Check if an Object is a Tabular Data Structure — is_tabular
code: is_tabular(x, warn = TRUE)

title: Check if an Object is a Tabular Data Structure — is_tabular
text: x R object test. warn Logical scalar. TRUE (default), informative warnings emitted x fails check, describing specific reason failure. Set FALSE suppress warnings obtain silent FALSE.

title: Check if an Object is a Tabular Data Structure — is_tabular
text: single logical value: TRUE x recognized tabular structure two dimensions, FALSE otherwise. return value never NA.

title: Check if an Object is a Tabular Data Structure — is_tabular
text: object considered tabular satisfies following conditions: class one "data.frame", "tbl_df", "tbl" (generic tibble superclass), "data.table", "matrix". exactly two dimensions (.e., length(dim(x)) == 2). class check uses inherits(), subclasses data.frame (e.g., tibbles, inherit "data.frame") naturally detected. matrix always two dimensions construction, dimension check applied uniformly consistency guard unusual edge cases zero-dimensional matrices produced certain external packages. NULL objects class attribute handled gracefully: always return FALSE. Note is_tabular() check whether object non-empty. zero-row zero-column tabular object still return TRUE, retains correct class dimensions. Callers require non-empty data perform check separately.

title: Check if an Object is a Tabular Data Structure — is_tabular
text: John Lennon L. Calorio

title: Check if an Object is a Tabular Data Structure — is_tabular
code: # --- TRUE cases ---  # Base data.frame is_tabular(mtcars) #> [1] TRUE  # Matrix is_tabular(matrix(1:12, nrow = 3)) #> [1] TRUE  # tibble (if tibble is installed) if (requireNamespace("tibble", quietly = TRUE)) {   is_tabular(tibble::tibble(x = 1:3, y = letters[1:3])) } #> [1] TRUE  # data.table (if data.table is installed) if (requireNamespace("data.table", quietly = TRUE)) {   is_tabular(data.table::data.table(a = 1:5, b = rnorm(5))) } #> [1] TRUE  # Zero-row data.frame - still tabular is_tabular(mtcars[0, ]) #> [1] TRUE  # --- FALSE cases ---  # Atomic vector is_tabular(1:10) #> Warning: 'x' has class <integer>, which is not a recognized tabular type. Expected one of: data.frame, tibble (tbl_df), data.table, or matrix. #> [1] FALSE  # List is_tabular(list(a = 1, b = 2)) #> Warning: 'x' has class <list>, which is not a recognized tabular type. Expected one of: data.frame, tibble (tbl_df), data.table, or matrix. #> [1] FALSE  # 3-D array (more than two dimensions) is_tabular(array(1:24, dim = c(2, 3, 4))) #> Warning: 'x' has class <array>, which is not a recognized tabular type. Expected one of: data.frame, tibble (tbl_df), data.table, or matrix. #> [1] FALSE  # NULL is_tabular(NULL) #> Warning: 'x' is NULL. A tabular object (data.frame, tibble, data.table, or matrix) was expected. #> [1] FALSE  # Suppress warnings for programmatic use is_tabular("not a table", warn = FALSE) #> [1] FALSE

what: Mean Intensity Heatmap with Hierarchical Clustering Dendrogram — plot_meanmap
title: Mean Intensity Heatmap with Hierarchical Clustering Dendrogram — plot_meanmap
text: Produces publication-ready mean intensity heatmap rows variables (e.g. metabolites) clustered hierarchical clustering columns group means derived grouping variable metadata. coloured dendrogram aligned left heatmap, optional cluster bracket annotations per-row significance markers right. function intended visualising group-level mean differences across variables, example, metabolite intensities across disease stages.

title: Mean Intensity Heatmap with Hierarchical Clustering Dendrogram — plot_meanmap
code: plot_meanmap(   x,   metadata = NULL,   group,   group_order = NULL,   sig_vars = NULL,   sig_marker = "*",   sig_color = "red",   clust_method = "ward.D2",   dist_method = "euclidean",   scale_vars = "none",   n_clusters = NULL,   cluster_labels = NULL,   cluster_colors = NULL,   heatmap_colors = c("#008000", "#FFFF00", "#FF0000"),   show_values = FALSE,   value_digits = 2L,   value_color = "black",   global_font_size = 11,   row_font_size = NULL,   col_font_size = NULL,   legend_font_size = NULL,   cluster_label_size = NULL,   sig_marker_size = NULL,   dend_width = 0.2,   legend_position = "right",   na_color = "grey80" )

title: Mean Intensity Heatmap with Hierarchical Clustering Dendrogram — plot_meanmap
text: x numeric matrix data frame whose columns variables (e.g. metabolites) whose rows observations/samples. Must column names. metadata data frame number rows x valid column names. Must contain column specified group. group single character string naming column metadata defines sample groups. Group means computed per level variable used heatmap columns. group_order character vector specifying display order group levels (left right). Must contain exactly levels metadata[[group]]. NULL (default), levels ordered appear factor(metadata[[group]]). sig_vars character vector variable (column) names mark significance marker right side heatmap. Default NULL (markers). sig_marker single character string used significance marker symbol. Default "*". sig_color single character string (colour name hex) significance marker. Default "red". clust_method single character string passed method argument hclust. Default "ward.D2". dist_method single character string passed method argument dist. Default "euclidean". scale_vars single character string controlling row-wise scaling applied clustering display. One "none" (default; use values supplied), "zscore" (subtract row mean, divide row SD), "minmax" (scale row (0, 1) inclusive). n_clusters positive integer NULL. NULL, dendrogram cut n_clusters clusters using cutree, cluster coloured distinct colour-blind-friendly palette, cluster bracket + label annotations added left dendrogram. Default NULL. cluster_labels character vector labels cluster, order top plot bottom (.e. dendrogram leaf order). Must length equal n_clusters supplied. Default NULL, auto-generates labels "Cluster ", "Cluster II", etc. cluster_colors character vector colours cluster. Must length equal n_clusters supplied. Default NULL, uses Okabe-Ito colour-blind-safe palette. heatmap_colors character vector length \(\ge 2\) specifying colour gradient heatmap fill, low high values. Default c("#008000", "#FFFF00", "#FF0000") (green–yellow–red). show_values Logical. Whether print rounded mean value inside heatmap cell. Default FALSE. value_digits single non-negative integer. Number decimal places -cell value labels. Default 2. Ignored show_values = FALSE. value_color single character string -cell text colour. Default "black". global_font_size Numeric. Base font size points. Default 11. row_font_size Numeric NULL. Font size row (variable) labels right y-axis. Derived global_font_size NULL (default). col_font_size Numeric NULL. Font size column (group) labels x-axis. Derived global_font_size NULL (default). legend_font_size Numeric NULL. Font size legend text. Derived global_font_size NULL (default). cluster_label_size Numeric NULL. Font size cluster bracket labels. Derived global_font_size NULL (default). Ignored n_clusters NULL. sig_marker_size Numeric NULL. Font size significance markers. Derived global_font_size NULL (default). dend_width Numeric (0, 1). Relative width dendrogram panel fraction total plot width. Default 0.2. legend_position single character string legend placement. One "right" (default), "bottom", "left", "top", "none". na_color single character string fill colour NA cells. Default "grey80".

title: Mean Intensity Heatmap with Hierarchical Clustering Dendrogram — plot_meanmap
text: patchwork object. following attributes attached: mean_matrix \(p \times g\) matrix group means (unscaled), rows dendrogram leaf order. scaled_matrix scaled version mean_matrix used clustering display (identical mean_matrix scale_vars = "none"). hclust_obj hclust object. leaf_order Integer vector row indices dendrogram leaf order. cluster_membership Named integer vector cluster assignments per variable (set n_clusters NULL).

title: Mean Intensity Heatmap with Hierarchical Clustering Dendrogram — plot_meanmap
text: Algorithm overview Group mean computation: level metadata[[group]], column means x computed (ignoring NAs), producing \(p \times g\) matrix \(p\) number variables \(g\) number groups. Optional scaling: scale_vars "zscore" "minmax", row (variable) mean matrix scaled independently clustering display. Clustering: Euclidean (user-specified) distances computed rows (possibly scaled) mean matrix hierarchical clustering applied. Reordering: Rows permuted leaf order dendrogram. Dendrogram: Built via ggdendro optional cluster colouring. bracket--label annotation layer added n_clusters supplied. Heatmap: Built ggplot2::geom_tile using user-controlled colour gradient. Significance markers -cell values added optional annotation layers. Assembly: patchwork::wrap_plots() aligns two panels zero inter-panel spacing. Scaling recommendation: variables comparable scales (e.g. z-scored log-transformed), scale_vars = "none" appropriate. raw intensities different scales, use scale_vars = "zscore" make colour gradient interpretable across rows.

title: Mean Intensity Heatmap with Hierarchical Clustering Dendrogram — plot_meanmap
code: # \donttest{ ## ── Basic example: iris mean intensity by species ──────────────────────── data(iris) num_part <- iris[, 1:4] meta_part <- data.frame(Species = iris$Species)  p <- plot_meanmap(   x         = num_part,   metadata  = meta_part,   group     = "Species",   scale_vars = "zscore" ) print(p)   ## ── With cluster colouring and significance markers ────────────────────── p2 <- plot_meanmap(   x                = num_part,   metadata         = meta_part,   group            = "Species",   scale_vars       = "zscore",   n_clusters       = 2,   cluster_labels   = c("Cluster I", "Cluster II"),   sig_vars         = c("Petal.Length", "Petal.Width"),   sig_color        = "red",   global_font_size = 11 ) print(p2)   ## ── Custom group order and colour palette ──────────────────────────────── p3 <- plot_meanmap(   x           = num_part,   metadata    = meta_part,   group       = "Species",   group_order = c("virginica", "versicolor", "setosa"),   scale_vars  = "zscore",   n_clusters  = 2,   heatmap_colors = c("#2166AC", "#F7F7F7", "#B2182B") ) print(p3)  # }

what: Plot Results from a Linear Mixed-Effects Model Analysis — plot_mixedmodel
title: Plot Results from a Linear Mixed-Effects Model Analysis — plot_mixedmodel
text: Produces volcano plot combined_fixed_effects component run_mixedmodel object, log2 fold-change (raw estimates) x-axis \(-\log_{10}(p_{\text{adj}})\) y-axis. model formula contained one fixed effect term, panels automatically faceted term predictor shown separately. Points colored significance direction, labels can added top-\(n\) features per panel user-specified set features.

title: Plot Results from a Linear Mixed-Effects Model Analysis — plot_mixedmodel
code: plot_mixedmodel(   x,   type = "volcano",   term = NULL,   use_padj = TRUE,   p_threshold = 0.05,   estimate_threshold = 0,   label_features = NULL,   minmax = NULL,   iqr_k = 1.5,   label_size = 3,   label_max_overlap = 20L,   label_box = FALSE,   label_color = "match",   point_size = 2,   point_size_sig = 2.5,   alpha = 0.6,   label_sig_only = TRUE,   color_up = "#D85A30",   color_down = "#0072B2",   color_ns = "#888780",   x_label = "Estimate (fixed effect)",   y_label = NULL,   title = NULL,   subtitle = NULL,   caption = NULL,   facet_scales = "fixed",   facet_ncol = NULL,   theme = "nature",   base_size = 12,   font_family = "",   zoom = 1 )

title: Plot Results from a Linear Mixed-Effects Model Analysis — plot_mixedmodel
text: x run_mixedmodel object returned run_mixedmodel. type Character string. Currently "volcano" supported. Reserved future types ("effect", "heatmap"). Default: "volcano". term character vector fixed-effect term name(s) include. NULL (default), non-intercept terms included. Useful focusing single predictor formula many. use_padj Logical. TRUE (default), y-axis reflects p_adj (Benjamini-Hochberg whichever method passed run_mixedmodel). FALSE, raw p_value used. p_threshold Numeric. Horizontal dashed significance line drawn \(-\log_{10}(\text{p\_threshold})\). Default: 0.05. estimate_threshold Numeric. Vertical dashed lines drawn \(\pm\)estimate_threshold. Set 0 suppress. Default: 0 (vertical lines). label_features character vector specific feature names label, regardless significance rank. Combined label_top (union). Default: NULL. minmax numeric vector length 2, e.g. c(-0.02, 0.02). Features estimate \(\le\) minmax[1] \(\ge\) minmax[2] annotated. Overrides automatic IQR-based annotation. Default: NULL. iqr_k Numeric. IQR fence multiplier used automatic annotation minmax label_features NULL. Features beyond \(Q1 - k \times IQR\) \(Q3 + k \times IQR\) labelled. Set Inf disable automatic annotation entirely. Default: 1.5. label_size Numeric. Font size point labels (ggplot2 pt units). Default: 3. label_max_overlap Integer. Passed ggrepel::geom_text_repel's max.overlaps. Default: 20. label_box Logical. TRUE, draws border box around labels via ggrepel::geom_label_repel. Default: FALSE. label_color Character. Color label text. "match" inherits point's significance color. valid color string overrides. Default: "match". point_size Numeric. Base point size non-significant features. Default: 2. point_size_sig Numeric. Point size significant features. Default: 2.5. alpha Numeric (0, 1]. Transparency non-significant points. Default: 0.6. label_sig_only Logical. TRUE (default), features passing p_threshold eligible minmax IQR-based auto-annotation. Features label_features always annotated regardless setting. Default: TRUE. color_up Character. Color significantly -regulated (positive estimate + significant) features. Default: "#D85A30" (Okabe-Ito vermillion). color_down Character. Color significantly -regulated (negative estimate + significant) features. Default: "#0072B2" (Okabe-Ito blue). color_ns Character. Color non-significant features. Default: "#888780". x_label Character. x-axis label. Default: "Estimate (fixed effect)". y_label Character. y-axis label. Default: expression(-log[10](p[adj])) expression(-log[10](p)) depending use_padj. title Character. Plot title. Default: NULL (title). subtitle Character. Plot subtitle. Default: NULL. caption Character. Plot caption. NULL produces automatic caption reporting p-value threshold adjustment method. "" suppresses caption entirely. Default: NULL. facet_scales Character. Passed ggplot2::facet_wrap's scales argument multiple terms plotted. One "fixed", "free_x", "free_y", "free". Default: "fixed". facet_ncol Integer NULL. Number columns facet grid. NULL lets ggplot2 decide. Default: NULL. theme Character string. One "nature" (default, built theme_bw), "minimal", "classic", "bw". base_size Numeric. Base font size passed theme. Default: 12. font_family Character. Font family. Default: "" (system default). zoom Numeric scalar. Multiplies point sizes, label sizes, line widths, base font size proportionally export scaling. Default: 1.

title: Plot Results from a Linear Mixed-Effects Model Analysis — plot_mixedmodel
text: Significance classes: feature assigned one three classes per term panel: — estimate > estimate_threshold p (adjusted raw) < p_threshold. — estimate < \(-\)estimate_threshold p < p_threshold. NS — meet either criterion. estimate_threshold = 0 (default), /split purely sign estimate — useful single continuous predictor (e.g., age, time numeric) conventional fold-change cutoff. ggrepel: ggrepel installed (strongly recommended dense plots), used automatically. Install install.packages("ggrepel"). absent, geom_text used slight upward nudge.

code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ result <- run_mixedmodel(   x        = x,   metadata = metadata,   formula  = ".feature ~ time + age + (1 | subject_id)" )  # Default: all terms, auto-label top 10 significant features plot_mixedmodel(result)  # Focus on one term, label specific features plot_mixedmodel(   result,   term           = "timepost",   label_features = c("glucose", "insulin"),   color_up       = "#CC79A7",   alpha          = 0.3,   alpha_sig      = 0.9,   title          = "Time effect (pre → post)" )  # No auto-labels, only highlight manually specified features plot_mixedmodel(   result,   label_features = c("glucose", "insulin", "PA O-28:1"),   label_box      = TRUE,   label_color    = "black" ) } # }

text: Dual-axis implementation. ggplot2 implements secondary axes linear rescalings primary axis (sec_axis). plot_multi_y computes linear transform mapping secondary variables' observed range primary range 5\ NA handling. title: Plot Odds Ratios — plot_oddsratio
text: Creates publication-ready odds ratio (forest) plot data frame containing odds ratios optional confidence interval bounds. Points colored according whether odds ratio , equal , 1 (null value). vertical reference line drawn = 1. confidence interval columns supplied, horizontal error bars added. function accepts data frames, matrices, tibbles, safely handles column names containing special characters (spaces, hyphens, parentheses, etc.).

text: Color logic odds ratio point colored comparing value 1: < 1  → first color colors (default "blue") = 1  → second color colors (default "grey") > 1  → third color colors (default "red") small numerical tolerance (.Machine$double.eps^0.5) applied testing exact equality 1. Axis centering x-axis expanded symmetrically around 1 log scale ensure 1 always sits visual center plot. makes immediately clear whether falls "decreased odds" "increased odds" half panel. Missing CI values Individual rows low /high NA error bars silently omitted; dot still plotted.

text: John Lennon L. Calorio

title: Plot Odds Ratios — plot_oddsratio
code: # ── Basic usage: OR only ───────────────────────────────────────────────────── df_basic <- data.frame(   feature  = c("Age", "BMI", "Smoking", "Hypertension", "Diabetes"),   OR       = c(1.45, 0.82, 2.31, 0.55, 1.02),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE ) rownames(df_basic) <- df_basic$feature  plot_oddsratio(x = df_basic, or = "OR")   # ── With confidence intervals ──────────────────────────────────────────────── df_ci <- data.frame(   feature  = c("Age", "BMI", "Smoking", "Hypertension", "Diabetes"),   OR       = c(1.45, 0.82, 2.31, 0.55, 1.02),   Lower95  = c(1.10, 0.61, 1.70, 0.31, 0.78),   Upper95  = c(1.91, 1.11, 3.14, 0.97, 1.33),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  plot_oddsratio(   x    = df_ci,   or   = "OR",   low  = "Lower95",   high = "Upper95",   ci   = 95,   feature_col = "feature",   plot_title  = "Logistic Regression: Odds Ratios" ) #> `height` was translated to `width`.   # ── Special-character column names ─────────────────────────────────────────── df_special <- data.frame(   check.names = FALSE,   "OR (adjusted)" = c(0.73, 1.88, 1.05),   "Lower 95% CI"  = c(0.51, 1.20, 0.79),   "Upper 95% CI"  = c(1.05, 2.94, 1.39) ) rownames(df_special) <- c("Metabolite A", "Metabolite B", "Metabolite C")  plot_oddsratio(   x    = df_special,   or   = "OR (adjusted)",   low  = "Lower 95% CI",   high = "Upper 95% CI" ) #> `height` was translated to `width`.   # ── Custom colors and theme ────────────────────────────────────────────────── plot_oddsratio(   x      = df_ci,   or     = "OR",   low    = "Lower95",   high   = "Upper95",   feature_col = "feature",   colors = c("#2166AC", "#878787", "#D6604D"),   theme  = "classic",   global_font_size = 13,   plot_title = "Forest Plot",   x_label    = "Odds Ratio (95% CI)",   y_label    = "Predictor" ) #> `height` was translated to `width`.

title: Plot Relative Abundance as Stacked Bar Chart — plot_relabund
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ library(pondeR) set.seed(42) n    <- 20 taxa <- c("Firmicutes", "Bacteroidetes", "Proteobacteria (class A)",           "Actino/bacteroides", "Verrucomicrobia", "Fusobacteria sp.",           "Tenericutes", "Spirochaetes", "Unknown", "Cyanobacteria",           "Chloroflexi", "Acidobacteria")  counts <- as.data.frame(   matrix(sample(0L:5000L, n * length(taxa), replace = TRUE),          nrow = n, dimnames = list(NULL, taxa)) ) meta <- data.frame(   SampleID = paste0("S", seq_len(n)),   Group    = rep(c("Control", "Treatment"), each = n / 2L) )  # --- Pipeline: col_rel_abundance -> CLR -> plot as-is --- norm_out <- run_normalize(counts, meta, method = "col_rel_abundance",                           verbose = FALSE) clr_out  <- run_transform(norm_out$data, method = "clr")  plot_relabund(   x                    = clr_out$data,   metadata             = meta,   limit                = 10L,   ignore_normalization = TRUE,   plot_title           = "Gut Microbiome (CLR-transformed)" )  # --- Group-aggregated from raw counts (group_aggregate = TRUE) --- plot_relabund(   x               = counts,   metadata        = meta,   show_x_as       = "Group",   group_aggregate = TRUE,   order           = c("Treatment", "Control"),   limit           = 8L,   sort            = "high",   plot_title      = "**Relative Abundance** by Group",   legend_title    = "Phylum" )  # --- Individual samples with group brackets (group_aggregate = FALSE) --- plot_relabund(   x                = counts,   metadata         = meta,   show_x_as        = "SampleID",   group_aggregate  = FALSE,   group_brackets   = Group ~ c("Control", "Treatment"),   limit            = 8L,   sort             = "high",   plot_title       = "Relative Abundance by Sample",   legend_title     = "Phylum" )  # --- Horizontal bars, individual samples, group brackets --- plot_relabund(   x                     = counts,   metadata              = meta,   show_x_as             = "SampleID",   group_aggregate       = FALSE,   group_brackets        = Group ~ c("Control", "Treatment"),   limit                 = 6L,   exclude_others        = FALSE,   unknown_before_others = TRUE,   abundance_in_y        = FALSE ) } # }

Color Defaults: default, discrete groups use Okabe-Ito palette, perceptually distinct safe common forms color vision deficiency (deuteranopia, protanopia, tritanopia). palette supports 8 groups. 8 groups, supply custom colors vector. Point Labeling: points_from parameter controls samples labeled: specified column exists metadata, values used labels. column exist, sequential labels (Sample_1, Sample_2, ...) used. Labels shown show_outliers = TRUE outlier samples . Common choices: "Sample", "SampleID", "SubjectID". Outlier Detection: Outliers identified using Mahalanobis distance group centroid. sample flagged outlier falls outside confidence region defined ellipse_level. default (0.95) identifies samples outside 95% confidence region. exactly mirrors ggplot2::stat_ellipse() behavior. Outlier detection requires: Categorical color_by ellipse = TRUE least 3 samples per group Sample Exclusion: exclude samples (e.g., QC samples) plot, exclude PCA computation using run_pca(exclude = ...) rather filtering plot stage. title: Volcano Plot for Fold Change and p-value Data — plot_volcano
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ ## ----------------------------------------------------------------------- ## Example 1 - minimal synthetic data, no group ## ----------------------------------------------------------------------- set.seed(42) n   <- 200 dat <- data.frame(   feature     = paste0("feat_", seq_len(n)),   fold_change = 2^rnorm(n, 0, 2),   pvalue      = runif(n, 0, 0.5) )  res <- plot_volcano(x = dat, y = "fold_change", z = "pvalue") print(res$plots[[1]])  ## ----------------------------------------------------------------------- ## Example 2 - asymmetric thresholds as raw fold changes, with labels ## ----------------------------------------------------------------------- ann <- setNames(c("feat_1", "feat_2", "feat_3"),                 c("Marker A", "Marker B", "Marker C"))  res2 <- plot_volcano(   x        = dat,   y        = "fold_change",   z        = "pvalue",   features = "feature",   up       = 1.5,   down     = 1/1.5,   annotate = ann ) print(res2$plots[[1]])  ## ----------------------------------------------------------------------- ## Example 3 - multi-group overlay ## ----------------------------------------------------------------------- set.seed(7) dat_multi <- data.frame(   feature    = rep(paste0("feat_", seq_len(50)), 3),   fold_change = 2^rnorm(150, 0, 1.5),   pvalue     = runif(150, 0, 0.3),   comparison = rep(c("Severe_vs_Healthy", "Nonsevere_vs_Healthy",                      "Severe_vs_Nonsevere"), each = 50) )  res3 <- plot_volcano(   x           = dat_multi,   y           = "fold_change",   z           = "pvalue",   group       = "comparison",   features    = "feature",   annotate    = setNames(c("feat_1", "feat_5"), c("Marker A", "Marker B")),   alpha       = 0.5,   legend_nrow = 2 ) print(res3$plots[[1]])  ## ----------------------------------------------------------------------- ## Example 4 - threshold-based annotation (annotate2) ## ----------------------------------------------------------------------- # Label everything with a 4-fold change that is also significant (p < 0.05) res4 <- plot_volcano(   x         = dat,   y         = "fold_change",   z         = "pvalue",   features  = "feature",   annotate2 = c(0.25, 4),   up        = 1.5,   down      = 0.5 ) print(res4$plots[[1]]) } # }

what: Compute WHO Anthropometric Z-Scores and Nutritional Status — run_anthroindex
title: Compute WHO Anthropometric Z-Scores and Nutritional Status — run_anthroindex
text: Computes Child Growth Standards z-scores eight anthropometric indices—weight--age (WFA), length/height--age (HFA), weight--length/height (WFH), BMI--age (BMIFA), head circumference--age (HCFA), arm circumference--age (ACFA), triceps skinfold--age (TSFA), subscapular skinfold--age (SSFA)—using anthro_zscores (Child Growth Standards 2006) ages 0 < 5 years, anthroplus_zscores (Growth Reference 2007) ages 5 < 20 years applicable. BMIFA, function covers full age range: 0 < 5 years: z-scores anthro_zscores, Child Growth Standards 2006. 5 < 20 years: z-scores anthroplus_zscores, Growth Reference 2007. anthroplus package must installed separately participants age range present. 20 years : nutritional status classified directly computed BMI value using adult cut-offs (z-score). Nutritional Status column appended result according classification thresholds.

title: Compute WHO Anthropometric Z-Scores and Nutritional Status — run_anthroindex
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ library(pondeR)  if (requireNamespace("anthro", quietly = TRUE)) {    # --- Synthetic dataset (no external data file required) ----------------   set.seed(42)   n <- 50   children <- data.frame(     child_id = seq_len(n),     sex      = sample(c("Male", "Female"), n, replace = TRUE),     age_mo   = runif(n, 1, 60),     wt_kg    = runif(n, 3, 20),     ht_cm    = runif(n, 50, 115),     oedema   = sample(c("n", "y"), n, replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.95, 0.05))   )    # --- Multiple indices — returns a named list ---------------------------   res <- run_anthroindex(     x               = children,     sex_col         = "sex",     age_col         = "age_mo",     age_unit        = "month",     weight_col      = "wt_kg",     weight_unit     = "kg",     height_col      = "ht_cm",     height_unit     = "cm",     height_measured = "L",     oedema_col      = "oedema",     return          = c("wfa", "hfa", "wfh", "bmifa")   )    head(res$wfa[,  c("z_score", "Nutritional Status")])   head(res$bmifa[, c("cbmi", "z_score", "Nutritional Status")])    # --- Single index — returns a plain data frame -------------------------   wfa_only <- run_anthroindex(     x               = children,     sex_col         = "sex",     age_col         = "age_mo",     age_unit        = "month",     weight_col      = "wt_kg",     weight_unit     = "kg",     height_col      = "ht_cm",     height_unit     = "cm",     height_measured = "L",     return          = "wfa"   )   head(wfa_only)    # --- Mixed-age dataset spanning all three age brackets ----------------   if (requireNamespace("anthroplus", quietly = TRUE)) {     mixed <- data.frame(       sex    = c("Male", "Female", "Male"),       age_yr = c(3, 14, 25),       wt_kg  = c(14, 38.8, 80),       ht_cm  = c(95, 151.13, 175)     )     bmi_res <- run_anthroindex(       x               = mixed,       sex_col         = "sex",       age_col         = "age_yr",       age_unit        = "year",       weight_col      = "wt_kg",       height_col      = "ht_cm",       height_measured = "H",       return          = "bmifa"     )     bmi_res[, c("age_yr", "cbmi", "z_score", "Nutritional Status")]   } } } # }

title: Automatic Statistical Comparison for Categorical Variables — run_assoc

title: Automatic Statistical Comparison for Categorical Variables — run_assoc
code: run_assoc(   x,   var1,   var2,   weight = NULL,   paired = FALSE,   force_categorical = FALSE,   test_type = c("auto", "chisq", "fisher"),   expected_freq_threshold = 5,   continuity_correction = TRUE,   fisher_simulate = FALSE,   test_alpha = 0.05,   calculate_association = TRUE,   perform_posthoc = TRUE,   p_adjust_method = "BH",   verbose = TRUE )

title: Automatic Statistical Comparison for Categorical Variables — run_assoc
text: x data frame containing variables analysis. var1 character vector one column names x specifying categorical variable(s) test. variable var1 independently paired var2 test association. listed columns must categorical (factor character), unless force_categorical = TRUE. var2 character string specifying name single categorical variable (typically grouping outcome column). variable var1 compared variable. weight optional character string specifying name weight column. Use x frequency-aggregated format (e.g., .data.frame.table()). paired logical indicating whether observations paired (McNemar's test). Default FALSE. force_categorical Logical. TRUE, column var1 var2 already factor character coerced factor analysis. useful numeric codes represent categorical groups (e.g., 0/1, 1/2/3). warning issued coerced column. Default FALSE. test_type character string specifying test strategy independent samples. One "auto" (default), "chisq", "fisher". Ignored paired = TRUE. expected_freq_threshold minimum expected cell frequency justify using Chi-square test test_type = "auto". Default 5. continuity_correction logical indicating whether apply Yates' continuity correction 2x2 Chi-square McNemar's tests. Default TRUE. fisher_simulate logical indicating whether use Monte Carlo simulation Fisher's Exact Test p-values. Recommended tables larger 2x2. Default FALSE. test_alpha Significance level final statistical test. Default 0.05. calculate_association Logical. Whether calculate measures association (Cramer's V, Phi, Odds Ratio). Requires effectsize. Default TRUE. perform_posthoc Logical. Whether perform post-hoc pairwise Fisher's tests significant tables one binary dimension. Requires rstatix. Default TRUE. p_adjust_method P-value adjustment method post-hoc comparisons. See p.adjust. Default "BH". verbose logical indicating whether print detailed messages. Default TRUE.

title: Automatic Statistical Comparison for Categorical Variables — run_assoc
text: list class "run_assoc" containing: test_used Character string statistical test performed. test_result htest object selected test. contingency_table Observed contingency table data frame. expected_table Expected frequencies data frame (NULL Fisher's/McNemar's). residuals Pearson residuals data frame (NULL Fisher's/McNemar's). stdres Standardized residuals data frame (NULL Fisher's/McNemar's). Values beyond \u00b1 1.96 \u00b1 2.58 indicate cells contributing significance. association Data frame association metrics (metric, estimate, CI, magnitude, interpretation), NULL. posthoc_result Data frame post-hoc pairwise comparisons, NULL. assumptions Data frame summarising assumption check results. var1 Name row variable. var2 Name column variable. paired paired setting used. test_alpha significance level used. warnings Character vector warnings generated. parameters List parameters used analysis.

title: Automatic Statistical Comparison for Categorical Variables — run_assoc
text: Automatic Statistical Comparison Categorical Variables Test Family Selection: paired = TRUE, requires 2x2 table performs McNemar's Test. paired = FALSE, assumes independent samples builds contingency table. Test Selection Logic (test_type = "auto" independent samples): choice Chi-square Fisher's test based Cochran's rule. function first calculates expected frequencies via Chi-square check. cell expected frequency expected_freq_threshold (default 5), Fisher's Exact Test used. Otherwise, Pearson's Chi-square Test used. Post-Hoc Tests: significant result table one dimension binary (2 x C R x 2), pairwise Fisher's tests performed via rstatix::pairwise_fisher_test. P-values adjusted using p_adjust_method. Measures Association (Effect Sizes): Phi Odds Ratio 2x2 independent tables. Cramer's V RxC independent tables (larger 2x2). Odds Ratio (paired) 2x2 paired (McNemar's) tables.

title: Automatic Statistical Comparison for Categorical Variables — run_assoc
text: John Lennon L. Calorio

title: Automatic Statistical Comparison for Categorical Variables — run_assoc
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # --- Example 1: 2x2 Independent Test (Auto -> Chi-square) --- data(mtcars) res <- run_assoc(x = mtcars, var1 = "am", var2 = "vs",                  force_categorical = TRUE) print(res) summary(res)  # --- Example 2: RxC Independent Test --- data(HairEyeColor) he <- as.data.frame.table(HairEyeColor) he_male <- he[he$Sex == "Male", ] res_hair <- run_assoc(x = he_male, var1 = "Hair", var2 = "Eye", weight = "Freq") summary(res_hair)  # --- Example 3: Paired 2x2 (McNemar's) --- set.seed(42) N <- 100 before <- sample(c("No", "Yes"), N, replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.7, 0.3)) after  <- before after[before == "No"][1:14]  <- "Yes" after[before == "Yes"][1:3]  <- "No" paired_df <- data.frame(before = before, after = after) res_paired <- run_assoc(x = paired_df, var1 = "before", var2 = "after",                          paired = TRUE) summary(res_paired)  # --- Example 4: Force Chi-square --- res_chisq <- run_assoc(x = mtcars, var1 = "am", var2 = "vs",                         test_type = "chisq", continuity_correction = TRUE,                         force_categorical = TRUE) print(res_chisq) } # }

title: Comprehensive Area Under the ROC Curve (AUROC) Analysis — run_auc
text: Workflow overview x coerced data.frame; columns rows subset according include_cols / include_rows. y values listed remove dropped, leaving exactly two groups. y categorical, two remaining levels mapped 0 (control, specified set_control) 1 (case). y already encodes 0/1 character factor, silently converted numeric. pROC::roc() called predictor column; AUC , optionally, DeLong confidence intervals (pROC::ci()) extracted. Plots assembled ggplot2 using colour-blind-friendly palette (Okabe-Ito). AUC labels rounded two decimal places; values round 1.00 progressively displayed decimal places (five), scientific notation two significant figures used. Reference line grey dashed diagonal line representing AUC 0.5 (random classifier) always drawn. Legend placement combined plots (plot = "") legend positioned bottom-right inside plot area default. pROC dependency function requires pROC (\(\ge\) 1.18) ggplot2 (\(\ge\) 3.4). listed Imports package DESCRIPTION file.

title: Comprehensive Area Under the ROC Curve (AUROC) Analysis — run_auc
text: John Lennon L. Calorio

title: Comprehensive Area Under the ROC Curve (AUROC) Analysis — run_auc
code: ## ----------------------------------------------------------------------- ## Example 1 - numeric binary response (0/1), all predictors ## ----------------------------------------------------------------------- set.seed(42) n  <- 120 df <- data.frame(   group    = sample(0:1, n, replace = TRUE),   marker_A = rnorm(n, mean = ifelse(sample(0:1, n, replace = TRUE) == 1, 1, 0)),   marker_B = rnorm(n),   marker_C = runif(n) )  res <- run_auc(x = df, y = "group", plot = "none") print(res$auc_table) #>   predictor       auc  ci_lower  ci_upper ci_level direction n_cases n_controls #> 1  marker_A 0.5133765 0.4085016 0.6182514     0.95         >      67         53 #> 2  marker_B 0.5243593 0.4185301 0.6301886     0.95         <      67         53 #> 3  marker_C 0.5350605 0.4300641 0.6400569     0.95         >      67         53  ## ----------------------------------------------------------------------- ## Example 2 - categorical response, remove one group, combined plot ## ----------------------------------------------------------------------- df2 <- data.frame(   status   = sample(c("control", "case", "unknown"), n, replace = TRUE),   score_1  = rnorm(n),   score_2  = rnorm(n) )  res2 <- run_auc(   x           = df2,   y           = "status",   remove      = "unknown",   set_control = "control",   plot        = "none",   compute_ci  = FALSE ) #> Moving non-numeric columns to metadata (predictors must be numeric): status print(res2$auc_table) #>   predictor       auc ci_lower ci_upper ci_level direction n_cases n_controls #> 1   score_1 0.5257453       NA       NA     0.95         >      36         41 #> 2   score_2 0.5372629       NA       NA     0.95         <      36         41  ## ----------------------------------------------------------------------- ## Example 3 - column names with spaces, select top-2 by AUC ## ----------------------------------------------------------------------- df3 <- data.frame(   `my group`  = sample(0:1, n, replace = TRUE),   `feature 1` = rnorm(n, mean = 0.5),   `feature 2` = rnorm(n),   check.names = FALSE )  res3 <- run_auc(   x    = df3,   y    = "my group",   plot = "none" ) print(res3$auc_table) #>   predictor       auc  ci_lower  ci_upper ci_level direction n_cases n_controls #> 1 feature 1 0.5167558 0.4120382 0.6214735     0.95         >      67         53 #> 2 feature 2 0.5516756 0.4461648 0.6571863     0.95         >      67         53

what: Run Lawshe's Content Validity Ratio (CVR) Analysis — run_contentvaliditycvr
title: Run Lawshe's Content Validity Ratio (CVR) Analysis — run_contentvaliditycvr
text: Computes Lawshe's (1975) Content Validity Ratio (CVR) one items rated expert panel. expert classifies item "essential", "useful essential", "necessary"; CVR captures proportion experts endorsing "essential" relative chance. function returns tidy data frame CVR values, critical values, significance interpretations item.

title: Run Lawshe's Content Validity Ratio (CVR) Analysis — run_contentvaliditycvr
code: run_contentvaliditycvr(   x,   essential = 1,   n = dim(x)[1L],   alpha = 0.05,   na.rm = FALSE )

title: Run Lawshe's Content Validity Ratio (CVR) Analysis — run_contentvaliditycvr
text: x numeric data frame matrix rows represent experts columns represent items. essential numeric vector indicating rating value(s) classify item "essential". Defaults 1. vector may supplied (e.g., c(1, 2)) multiple adjacent categories count essential. n integer specifying panel size used compute minimum CVR considered statistically significant via cvr_critical(). Defaults dim(x)[1] (number rows x). alpha numeric value one-tailed significance level used cvr_critical(). Defaults 0.05. na.rm Logical. TRUE, missing ratings excluded counting experts per item. Defaults FALSE.

title: Run Lawshe's Content Validity Ratio (CVR) Analysis — run_contentvaliditycvr
text: data frame class run_contentvaliditycvr one row per item following columns: CVR Lawshe's Content Validity Ratio. Critical Value Minimum CVR statistical significance specified alpha panel size n. Interpretation Character; "Significant" CVR >= Critical Value, otherwise "significant".

title: Run Lawshe's Content Validity Ratio (CVR) Analysis — run_contentvaliditycvr
text: CVR defined : $$\text{CVR} = \frac{n_e - n/2}{n/2}$$ \(n_e\) number experts rating item "essential" \(n\) total number experts. CVR ranges \(-1\) (expert endorses "essential") \(+1\) (experts endorse "essential"). value \(0\) indicates exactly half panel rated item "essential". Statistical significance CVR evaluated critical value returned contentValidity::cvr_critical(n, alpha), implements recalculated critical values Wilson, Pan, Schumsky (2012).

title: Run Lawshe's Content Validity Ratio (CVR) Analysis — run_contentvaliditycvr
text: Lawshe, C. H. (1975). quantitative approach content validity. Personnel Psychology, 28(4), 563–575. doi:10.1111/j.1744-6570.1975.tb01393.x Wilson, F. R., Pan, W., & Schumsky, D. . (2012). Recalculation critical values Lawshe's content validity ratio. Measurement Evaluation Counseling Development, 45(3), 197–210. doi:10.1177/0748175612440286

title: Run Lawshe's Content Validity Ratio (CVR) Analysis — run_contentvaliditycvr
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Lawshe's (1975) original 3-point scale: # 1 = essential, 2 = useful but not essential, 3 = not necessary ratings <- data.frame(   item1 = c(1, 1, 1, 2, 1),   item2 = c(3, 3, 1, 3, 3),   item3 = c(1, 1, 1, 1, 1) ) result <- run_contentvaliditycvr(ratings, essential = 1) result  # Treat both "essential" and "useful but not essential" as endorsing the item result2 <- run_contentvaliditycvr(ratings, essential = c(1, 2)) result2 } # }

title: Automatic Statistical Comparison with Comprehensive Analysis — run_diff
text: Automatic Statistical Comparison Comprehensive Analysis Test Family Selection: paired = FALSE, independent groups compared. 2 groups independent paired two-sample test chosen; > 2 groups ANOVA-family test used. Assumption Checking (test_type = "auto"): Normality: Shapiro-Wilk (n < 50) Lilliefors (n ≥ 50) "auto". Per-group 2-group comparisons; model residuals > 2 groups. Homogeneity Variance: Levene's test (car); falls back Bartlett's test car unavailable. Test Selection Logic (test_type = "auto"): Independent two-sample: Mann-Whitney U (normality violated) → Welch's t (variance violated) → Student's t. Paired two-sample: Wilcoxon signed-rank (normality violated) → Paired t. Independent > 2 groups: Kruskal-Wallis (normality violated) → Welch's ANOVA (variance violated) → One-way ANOVA. Post-Hoc Tests (> 2 groups, significant omnibus): Tukey HSD (ANOVA), Games-Howell (Welch's ANOVA), Dunn's test (Kruskal-Wallis), Pairwise t-test (RM / Mixed ANOVA). Effect Sizes: Cohen's d / paired Cohen's d (t-tests), rank-biserial correlation (Wilcoxon/Mann-Whitney), eta-squared (ANOVA), epsilon-squared (Kruskal-Wallis), generalized partial eta-squared (RM / Mixed ANOVA). Non-Parametric Interpretation (Stochastic Dominance): non-parametric tests interpretation column reads "X tends larger/smaller values Y".

title: Fold Change Analysis Across Groups — run_foldchange
text: Fold change computation Group means computed per feature. \(k\) ordered levels \(g_1, g_2, \ldots, g_k\), fold changes computed pairwise combinations taken left--right arrange: \(\bar{x}_{g_i} / \bar{x}_{g_j}\) \(< j\), yielding \(k(k-1)/2\) comparisons (e.g., A_vs_B, A_vs_C, B_vs_C 3 groups). Log2 shifting log2 = TRUE, let \(m\) global minimum (filtered, selected) data matrix. shift \(\delta = \text{eps} - m\) added every value \(m \le 0\); otherwise shift applied (\(\delta = 0\)). Log2 fold changes computed shifted group means. Integration run_diff plot_volcano returned list exposes fc_table, log2fc_table, shifted_data consistent format plot_volcano() can consume directly.

title: Fold Change Analysis Across Groups — run_foldchange
text: John Lennon L. Calorio

title: Fold Change Analysis Across Groups — run_foldchange
code: ## ----------------------------------------------------------------------- ## Example 1 - basic usage with iris ## ----------------------------------------------------------------------- data(iris) x_iris  <- iris[, 1:4] meta_iris <- data.frame(Species = iris$Species)  res <- run_foldchange(   x        = x_iris,   metadata = meta_iris,   group    = "Species" ) print(res$fc_table) #>        feature setosa_vs_versicolor setosa_vs_virginica versicolor_vs_virginica #> 1  Sepal.Width            1.2375451           1.1526564               0.9314055 #> 2 Sepal.Length            0.8433288           0.7598664               0.9010322 #> 3 Petal.Length            0.3431925           0.2633285               0.7672911 #> 4  Petal.Width            0.1855204           0.1214215               0.6544916 print(res$log2fc_table) #>        feature setosa_vs_versicolor setosa_vs_virginica versicolor_vs_virginica #> 1  Sepal.Width            0.3074811           0.2049625              -0.1025187 #> 2 Sepal.Length           -0.2458328          -0.3961823              -0.1503495 #> 3 Petal.Length           -1.5429101          -1.9250643              -0.3821541 #> 4  Petal.Width           -2.4303506          -3.0419040              -0.6115534  ## ----------------------------------------------------------------------- ## Example 2 - custom order, filter one level, select two features ## ----------------------------------------------------------------------- res2 <- run_foldchange(   x        = x_iris,   metadata = meta_iris,   group    = "Species",   arrange  = c("virginica", "versicolor", "setosa"),   filter   = NULL,   select   = c("Sepal.Length", "Petal.Length") ) print(res2$comparisons) #> [1] "virginica_vs_versicolor" "virginica_vs_setosa"     #> [3] "versicolor_vs_setosa"    print(res2$fc_table) #>        feature virginica_vs_versicolor virginica_vs_setosa versicolor_vs_setosa #> 1 Petal.Length                1.303286            3.797538             2.913817 #> 2 Sepal.Length                1.109838            1.316021             1.185777  ## ----------------------------------------------------------------------- ## Example 3 - data with special characters in column names ## ----------------------------------------------------------------------- set.seed(1) n   <- 60 xsc <- data.frame(   `LPC 18:2`  = rnorm(n, 5, 1),   `PA O-28:1` = rnorm(n, 3, 0.5),   check.names = FALSE ) meta_sc <- data.frame(group = rep(c("Healthy", "Disease"), each = 30))  res3 <- run_foldchange(x = xsc, metadata = meta_sc, group = "group") print(res3$fc_table) #>     feature Disease_vs_Healthy #> 1  LPC 18:2             1.0099 #> 2 PA O-28:1             1.0005

text: x Matrix data frame. Numeric data samples rows features columns. Can output run_scale() raw data (scaled scale_method specified). metadata Data frame. Sample metadata number rows equal nrow(x). Must contain columns grouping, batch information, sample identifiers. scale_method Character NULL. Scaling method apply PCA x already scaled. Options: "mean", "auto", "pareto". NULL (default) x run_scale(), warning issued PCA proceeds without additional scaling. transform_method Character NULL. Transformation method apply scaling PCA. Options: "log2", "log10", "sqrt", "cbrt", "vsn", "glog". Default: NULL (transformation). group Character. Name column metadata containing group labels sample exclusion. Required exclude specified. Default: "Group". exclude Character vector NULL. Group labels (group column) exclude PCA analysis. example, c("QC", "Blank") excludes samples group labels. text: list containing: scores Matrix PC scores (samples × PCs) loadings Matrix PC loadings (features × PCs) variance_explained Numeric vector variance explained (%) per PC eigenvalues Numeric vector eigenvalues per PC cumulative_variance Numeric vector cumulative variance explained (%) per PC center_values Numeric vector centering values used () scale_values Numeric vector scaling values used () pca_object Original prcomp object stats::prcomp data_used Matrix data used PCA (transformation/scaling) metadata Data frame metadata aligned PCA scores n_samples Integer. Number samples used PCA n_samples_excluded Integer. Number samples excluded n_features Integer. Number features n_pcs Integer. Number PCs computed excluded_groups Character vector excluded group labels preprocessing List documenting preprocessing steps applied parameters List parameters used

title: Perform Principal Component Analysis — run_pca
text: Sample Exclusion: exclude parameter allows removal specific sample groups PCA: Common use: Exclude QC samples (exclude = c("QC", "SQC", "EQC")) Multiple groups can excluded: exclude = c("QC", "Blank", "Failed") Exclusion performed preprocessing steps Excluded samples included transformation/scaling calculations Input Data Requirements: function accepts data two forms: Pre-scaled data run_scale(): PCA performed directly without additional preprocessing. recommended workflow. Raw data: scale_method specified, function first apply optional transformation (via transform_method), scaling (via scale_method), performing PCA. PCA Methodology: PCA performed using singular value decomposition (SVD) via stats::prcomp(). function assumes data appropriately preprocessed (normalized, transformed, scaled) analysis. PCA performed without additional centering scaling (center = FALSE, scale. = FALSE) applied scaling step. Interpretation: PC Scores: Coordinates samples principal component space. Used visualization identifying patterns/clusters. PC Loadings: Contribution feature principal component. Features high absolute loadings drive separation along PC. Variance Explained: Percentage total variance captured PC. First PCs typically capture variance metabolomics data. Eigenvalues: Variance captured PC original units. Eigenvalue > 1 (Kaiser criterion) often used determine significant PCs. Scaling Recommendations: Auto-scaling ("auto"): Recommended PCA. Gives equal weight features regardless magnitude. Pareto-scaling ("pareto"): Alternative partially preserves variance structure. Can useful feature magnitudes carry biological meaning. Mean-centering ("mean"): centers data. Use features already comparable scales.

code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Simulate data set.seed(123) x <- matrix(rnorm(100 * 50, mean = 100, sd = 20),             nrow = 100, ncol = 50) colnames(x) <- paste0("Feature", 1:50) rownames(x) <- paste0("Sample", 1:100)  metadata <- data.frame(   Sample = paste0("Sample", 1:100),   Group = rep(c("Control", "Treatment", "QC"), c(40, 40, 20)),   Batch = rep(1:4, each = 25),   Time = rep(c(0, 6, 12, 24), 25) )  # PCA on pre-scaled data (recommended) scaled_result <- run_scale(x, method = "auto") pca_result <- run_pca(scaled_result$data, metadata)  # PCA excluding QC samples pca_result2 <- run_pca(scaled_result$data, metadata,                         group = "Group",                        exclude = "QC")  # PCA with automatic scaling, excluding multiple groups pca_result3 <- run_pca(x, metadata,                         scale_method = "auto",                        group = "Group",                        exclude = c("QC", "Blank"))  # PCA with transformation and scaling pca_result4 <- run_pca(x, metadata,                         transform_method = "log2",                        scale_method = "pareto",                        group = "Group",                        exclude = "QC")  # View results print(pca_result) summary(pca_result) } # }

title: Principal Coordinate Analysis (PCoA) with Optional PERMANOVA and Post-hoc — run_pcoa
text: Performs Principal Coordinate Analysis (PCoA), also known metric multidimensional scaling (MDS), sequential wrapper around vegdist pcoa. Optionally runs Permutational Multivariate Analysis Variance (PERMANOVA) via adonis2 dissimilarity matrix. Can also perform pairwise post-hoc testing using OmicFlow::pairwise_adonis. analyses share single dissimilarity matrix method applied exactly .

code: ## -- Example 1: Bray-Curtis PCoA on the dune dataset ---------------------- if (requireNamespace("vegan", quietly = TRUE) &&     requireNamespace("ape",   quietly = TRUE)) {    data(dune,     package = "vegan")   data(dune.env, package = "vegan")    result <- run_pcoa(dune, method = "bray")   print(result)   summary(result)   head(result$scores[, c("PC1", "PC2")])   result$variance_explained[1:3] } #> Warning: 5 negative eigenvalue(s) detected in the PCoA decomposition. #>   This is common with non-Euclidean dissimilarity indices (e.g., Bray-Curtis). #>   Consider correction = "lingoes" or "cailliez" for a fully Euclidean representation. #> Principal Coordinate Analysis (PCoA) #> ----------------------------------------  #> Dissimilarity method : bray  #> Correction           : none  #> Observations         : 20  #> Axes retained        : 14  #>  #> Variance explained (first 5 axes): #>   PC1: 37.36% #>   PC2: 22.26% #>   PC3: 10.05% #>   PC4: 8.32% #>   PC5: 6.08% #>  #> =======================================================  #> Principal Coordinate Analysis - Summary #> =======================================================  #>  #> Call: #>    run_pcoa(x = dune, method = "bray")  #>  #> Dissimilarity method :  bray  #> Correction           :  none  #> Observations (n)     :  20  #> Total axes           :  14  #>  #> Eigenvalues and variance explained (first 5 axes): #>  Axis Eigenvalue Variance_pct Cumulative_pct #>   PC1    1.71627       37.359         37.359 #>   PC2    1.02240       22.255         59.615 #>   PC3    0.46146       10.045         69.660 #>   PC4    0.38225        8.321         77.980 #>   PC5    0.27913        6.076         84.057 #>  #> =======================================================  #>      PC1      PC2      PC3  #> 37.35930 22.25533 10.04505   ## -- Example 2: PCoA + PERMANOVA ------------------------------------------- if (requireNamespace("vegan", quietly = TRUE) &&     requireNamespace("ape",   quietly = TRUE)) {    data(dune,     package = "vegan")   data(dune.env, package = "vegan")    result_perm <- run_pcoa(     x        = dune,     method   = "bray",     metadata = dune.env,     rhs      = "Management",     perms    = 999   )    # Extract PERMANOVA results individually   result_perm$permanova$R2   result_perm$permanova$p_value   result_perm$permanova$table } #> Warning: 5 negative eigenvalue(s) detected in the PCoA decomposition. #>   This is common with non-Euclidean dissimilarity indices (e.g., Bray-Curtis). #>   Consider correction = "lingoes" or "cailliez" for a fully Euclidean representation. #> Warning: number of items to replace is not a multiple of replacement length #>            Df SumOfSqs        R2        F Pr(>F) #> Management  3 1.468592 0.3416107 2.767243  0.002 #> Residual   16 2.830430 0.6583893       NA     NA #> Total      19 4.299022 1.0000000       NA     NA  ## -- Example 3: Lingoes correction ----------------------------------------- if (requireNamespace("vegan", quietly = TRUE) &&     requireNamespace("ape",   quietly = TRUE)) {    data(dune, package = "vegan")   result_ling <- run_pcoa(dune, method = "bray", correction = "lingoes")   result_ling$variance_explained[1:3] } #>       PC1       PC2       PC3  #> 29.538396 18.233837  9.095053   ## -- Example 4: Precomputed dist + PERMANOVA ------------------------------- if (requireNamespace("vegan", quietly = TRUE) &&     requireNamespace("ape",   quietly = TRUE)) {    data(dune,     package = "vegan")   data(dune.env, package = "vegan")   d <- vegan::vegdist(dune, method = "jaccard")    result_dist <- run_pcoa(     x        = d,     metadata = dune.env,     rhs      = "Management",     perms    = 999   )   result_dist$method          # "user-supplied dist"   result_dist$permanova$R2 } #> Warning: 1 negative eigenvalue(s) detected in the PCoA decomposition. #>   This is common with non-Euclidean dissimilarity indices (e.g., Bray-Curtis). #>   Consider correction = "lingoes" or "cailliez" for a fully Euclidean representation. #> Warning: number of items to replace is not a multiple of replacement length #> [1] 0.2943778

what: Randomize a Data Frame, Matrix, or Vector — run_randomize
title: Randomize a Data Frame, Matrix, or Vector — run_randomize
text: Randomly samples rows data frame, matrix, elements vector, optional group-stratified sampling. Serves structured wrapper around slice_sample, extending flexible per-group control (joint sequential/independent stratification), reproducible seeding, strict input validation suitable biological clinical research workflows.

title: Randomize a Data Frame, Matrix, or Vector — run_randomize
code: run_randomize(   x,   n = NULL,   p = NULL,   per = c("all", "row", "col"),   per_group = NULL,   joint = TRUE,   joint_behaviour = "cascading",   replace = FALSE,   seed = 123L )

title: Randomize a Data Frame, Matrix, or Vector — run_randomize
text: x data frame, tibble, matrix, atomic vector. Column names special characters supported. Matrices coerced data frames internally result recast matrix return. n single positive integer specifying number rows (elements, x vector) return. Defaults NULL. Mutually exclusive p; supplied, n takes priority warning issued. per_group supplied without explicit n/p inside list, top-level value used fallback. p single numeric value [0, 1] specifying proportion rows (elements) return. Defaults NULL (equivalent p = 1, .e., rows shuffled). Mutually exclusive n. per_group supplied without explicit n/p inside list, top-level value used fallback. per character string specifying randomization strategy data frames, matrices, tibbles. "" (default): randomize items data frame. value can placed anywhere randomized data frame. "row": randomize items row-wise. randomization done row independently. "col": randomize items column-wise. randomization done column independently. per_group optional list per-group sampling specs controlling stratified sampling. element must follow one two forms: Single column: list("ColumnName", n = <integer>) list("ColumnName", p = <number>) Multiple columns (joint independent): list(c("Col1", "Col2"), n = <integer>) list(c("Col1", "Col2"), p = <number>) list(c("Col1", "Col2"), n = c(2, 3)) (per-column values, joint = FALSE + joint_behaviour = "independent" ) n/p inside list element optional. omitted, top-level n p argument used fallback (top-level n takes priority top-level p provided). n p supplied inside list element, n takes priority warning issued. Requires x data frame matrix. Grouping columns must categorical (character, factor, logical). See joint joint_behaviour multiple columns handled. joint single logical value. Defaults TRUE. TRUE: columns listed per_group entry treated simultaneously — rows grouped every unique combination levels across columns (via interaction), n/p enforced combination. example, cyl gear, temporary group key "6:4" "8:3" created internally discarded sampling. FALSE: joint_behaviour argument controls whether columns handled cascading independent fashion. Ignored single column listed per_group entry. joint_behaviour character string, either "cascading" ("c") "independent" (""). applies joint = FALSE per_group entry lists multiple columns. Defaults "cascading". "cascading": columns applied sequentially order listed. data first grouped column 1 sampled; reduced result grouped column 2 sampled , . step's n/p applied whatever rows remain previous step, can aggressively reduce output n small. "independent": column sampled independently original x. results combined taking union selected row indices, row qualifies chosen grouping columns. per-column n/p vectors supplied (e.g., n = c(2, 3)), value corresponds positionally column. Vectors must exceed number columns length. replace single logical value. FALSE (default), sampling performed without replacement. TRUE, rows elements may repeated output. seed single integer passed set.seed reproducibility. Defaults 123L. Supply NA skip seeding (non-reproducible runs).

title: Randomize a Data Frame, Matrix, or Vector — run_randomize
text: object class x: Data frame / tibble: data frame tibble sampled rows, preserving columns classes. Row names dropped (consistent slice_sample behaviour). Matrix: matrix sampled rows, preserving column names storage mode. Vector: atomic vector sampled elements, preserving names present.

title: Randomize a Data Frame, Matrix, or Vector — run_randomize
text: run_randomize() operates three mutually exclusive top-level modes, resolved following order priority: Per-group stratified sampling (per_group NULL): Rows sampled within groups defined column(s) per_group entry. joint joint_behaviour arguments control multiple columns handled (see ). Fixed-count sampling (n supplied, per_group =     NULL): Exactly n rows returned. Setting n equal nrow(x) returns rows new random order. Proportional sampling (p supplied, n p omitted): fraction p rows returned. Omitting defaults p = 1 (full shuffle). p = 0 returns zero-row object class.

text: John Lennon L. Calorio

title: Reduce Multiple Data Frames by Common or Unique Column/Row Names — run_reduce
code: ## --- Example data --------------------------------------------------- df1 <- data.frame(a = 1:3, b = 4:6,  c = 7:9) df2 <- data.frame(a = 10:12, b = 13:15, d = 16:18) df3 <- data.frame(a = 19:21, b = 22:24, e = 25:27)  ## 1. Return columns common to ALL data frames (default) result_common <- run_reduce(df1, df2, df3) result_common$data          # columns: a, b #>    a  b #> 1  1  4 #> 2  2  5 #> 3  3  6 #> 4 10 13 #> 5 11 14 #> 6 12 15 #> 7 19 22 #> 8 20 23 #> 9 21 24 result_common$summary       # columns dropped per frame #> $df1 #> [1] "c" #>  #> $df2 #> [1] "d" #>  #> $df3 #> [1] "e" #>   ## 2. Return columns from ANY data frame (union, fill with NA) result_union <- run_reduce(df1, df2, df3, return_common = FALSE) result_union$data           # columns: a, b, c, d, e  (NAs where absent) #>    a  b  c  d  e #> 1  1  4  7 NA NA #> 2  2  5  8 NA NA #> 3  3  6  9 NA NA #> 4 10 13 NA 16 NA #> 5 11 14 NA 17 NA #> 6 12 15 NA 18 NA #> 7 19 22 NA NA 25 #> 8 20 23 NA NA 26 #> 9 21 24 NA NA 27  ## 3. Row-wise reduction ------------------------------------------- m1 <- data.frame(x = c(1, 2, 3), row.names = c("r1", "r2", "r3")) m2 <- data.frame(y = c(4, 5, 6), row.names = c("r1", "r3", "r4"))  result_rows <- run_reduce(m1, m2, check_cols = FALSE) result_rows$data            # rows: r1, r3 (common to both) #>    x y #> r1 1 4 #> r3 3 5  result_rows_union <- run_reduce(m1, m2, check_cols = FALSE,                                 return_common = FALSE) result_rows_union$data      # rows: r1, r2, r3, r4 (NAs where absent) #>     x  y #> r1  1  4 #> r2  2 NA #> r3  3  5 #> r4 NA  6  ## 4. Single data frame (warning, returned as-is) ------------------ result_single <- run_reduce(df1) #> Warning: Only one data frame was supplied. Returning it unchanged (no reduction performed).

title: Regularized Regression with Cross-Validation (Ridge, Elastic Net, LASSO) — run_regreg
text: Fits regularized regression models — Ridge (alpha = 0), LASSO (alpha = 1), Elastic Net (0 < alpha < 1) — k-fold cross-validation via cv.glmnet. Supports categorical (binary multinomial classification) continuous (Gaussian regression) dependent variables. multiple alpha values supplied, evaluated identical cross-validation folds best-performing model returned alongside comparison table. Unpenalized covariates (e.g., batch indicators, demographic confounders) can included not_penalized argument; coefficients estimated freely predictors remain subject regularization.

title: Regularized Regression with Cross-Validation (Ridge, Elastic Net, LASSO) — run_regreg
text: x data.frame, tibble, matrix predictor variables. Must column names (special characters supported). columns treated features penalized unless overridden not_penalized. Rows must correspond samples metadata. metadata data.frame tibble containing sample-level metadata. Must number rows x order. Must contain column specified pred. pred single character string naming column metadata use dependent variable. column numeric, Gaussian regression performed. character factor, classification performed. ref single character string specifying reference category categorical pred. Required classification; ignored numeric pred. not_penalized Optional character vector column names metadata /x include model without penalization (penalty factor = 0). Useful known confounders covariates must controlled. column named pred automatically excluded warning accidentally included (e.g. passing names(metadata)). Default: NULL. is_numeric Optional character vector column names listed not_penalized treated already numeric, bypassing automatic non-numeric detection integer-code conversion. Use column genuinely numeric stored character factor (e.g. due upstream data import). names must exist metadata x must also appear not_penalized. Default: NULL. train_percent numeric scalar (0, 1) specifying proportion samples used model training. remaining 1 - train_percent held evaluation. Default: 0.8. alpha numeric scalar vector values [0, 1]: 0 Ridge regression (L2 penalty; retains predictors) 1 LASSO regression (L1 penalty; performs variable selection) (0, 1) Elastic Net (blend L1 L2) vector supplied values evaluated best model selected. Default: 0.5. lambda single string controlling lambda selection cross-validation: "1se" Lambda within one standard error minimum CV error (conservative; fewer selected predictors). Default. "min" Lambda minimizing CV error (less conservative; selected predictors). cv_folds positive integer 3 20 specifying number cross-validation folds. Default: 10. type_measure character string specifying CV loss function. NULL (default) selects automatically based pred type: Categorical "class" (misclassification error). Also accepts "auc" (binary ; falls back "class" multinomial) "deviance". Numeric "mse" (mean squared error). Also accepts "mae" "deviance". parallel Logical. TRUE, cross-validation parallelized using registered foreach backend (e.g., via doParallel::registerDoParallel()). warning issued TRUE backend registered. Default: FALSE. standardize Logical. Whether standardize predictors inside glmnet prior fitting. Set FALSE (default) data already preprocessed/scaled. Default: FALSE. maxit positive integer specifying maximum number iterations coordinate descent convergence. title: Regularized Regression with Cross-Validation (Ridge, Elastic Net, LASSO) — run_regreg
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ ## ---- Simulate data ------------------------------------------------------- set.seed(42) n  <- 80 p  <- 30  feat <- as.data.frame(   matrix(rnorm(n * p), nrow = n,          dimnames = list(NULL, paste0("feat_", seq_len(p)))) )  meta <- data.frame(   SampleID = paste0("S", seq_len(n)),   Group    = sample(c("Control", "Case"), n, replace = TRUE),   Age      = round(runif(n, 20, 65)) )  ## ---- Binary classification (Elastic Net, single alpha) ------------------- res_bin <- run_regreg(   x             = feat,   metadata      = meta,   pred          = "Group",   ref           = "Control",   alpha         = 0.5,   train_percent = 0.8,   seed          = 123 ) print(res_bin) res_bin$modelsummary res_bin$BestModel$Coefficients  ## ---- Binary classification (multiple alpha sweep) ------------------------ res_sweep <- run_regreg(   x             = feat,   metadata      = meta,   pred          = "Group",   ref           = "Control",   alpha         = c(0, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1),   lambda        = "1se",   seed          = 123 ) res_sweep$AlphaComparison  ## ---- LASSO with an unpenalized covariate --------------------------------- res_lasso <- run_regreg(   x             = feat,   metadata      = meta,   pred          = "Group",   ref           = "Control",   not_penalized = "Age",   alpha         = 1,   seed          = 123 )  ## ---- Gaussian regression (numeric pred) ---------------------------------- res_num <- run_regreg(   x             = feat,   metadata      = meta,   pred          = "Age",   alpha         = c(0, 0.5, 1),   train_percent = 0.75,   seed          = 7 ) res_num$BestModel$Performance  ## ---- Multinomial classification ------------------------------------------ meta3 <- meta meta3$Group <- sample(c("A", "B", "C"), n, replace = TRUE)  res_multi <- run_regreg(   x             = feat,   metadata      = meta3,   pred          = "Group",   ref           = "A",   alpha         = 0.5,   seed          = 1 ) res_multi$BestModel$Coefficients } # }

what: Balanced Subsetting of Rows or Columns — run_subset
title: Balanced Subsetting of Rows or Columns — run_subset
text: Subsets data frame, tibble, matrix either row-wise column-wise, optional balancing across categorical groupings. balancing requested via match, function ensures every category within specified column represented number observations. optional n argument acts row ceiling: per-category count derived chosen set close n possible remaining perfectly balanced within match column. match contains one column, independent argument controls combined: independent = TRUE (default): match column balanced separately. row selected satisfies balance requirement match column (union). maximises number chosen rows. independent = FALSE: match columns crossed single Cartesian product categories (e.g. "1-2 years × Male"), every unique combination represented equally. guarantees perfect joint balance.

title: Balanced Subsetting of Rows or Columns — run_subset
code: run_subset(   x,   n = NULL,   match = NULL,   independent = TRUE,   balance = "within_match",   group = NULL,   by = "row",   random = TRUE,   top_to_bottom = TRUE,   left_to_right = TRUE,   seed = 123 )

title: Balanced Subsetting of Rows or Columns — run_subset
text: x data frame, tibble, matrix subsetted. n integer specifying desired number rows (= "row") columns (= "col") $chosen. match NULL (default) character vector one column names x containing categorical variables balance . independent Logical; TRUE (default). match multiple columns: TRUE, column balanced independently results unioned. FALSE, columns crossed single interaction balanced jointly. balance string; either "within_match" (default) "within_group". Controls direction balancing match group provided. "within_match": every category match, levels group equal number observations. example, balancing Age Group, Age Group equal number Diseased Healthy patients. "within_group": every category group, levels match equal number observations. example, within Diseased group, equal number patients Age Group. group NULL (default) character vector column names define additional grouping strata. string; either "row" (default) "col". random Logical; TRUE (default) select rows columns randomly. top_to_bottom Logical; TRUE (default). deterministic row pulls. left_to_right Logical; TRUE (default). deterministic col pulls. seed integer; 123 (default). random seed. NULL disables seeding.
